did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and machine learning methods for automated melanoma detection and lesion segmentation in dermatology. Specifically, it employed an ensemble of deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161) for classification and U-Net architectures (including U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder) for segmentation. It also utilized the XGBoost machine learning algorithm for classification tasks. The models were trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Challenge dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161, U-Net) for automated melanoma detection and lesion segmentation. It describes specific methods, datasets (ISIC 2017), implementation details, and reports new results including performance metrics (AUC, Jaccard Index, Dice Coefficient) and competition rankings. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICSSE 2019), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Lesion Segmentation and Automated Melanoma Detection using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks and XGBoost
journal: 2019 International Conference on System Science and Engineering (ICSSE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the subjectivity in melanoma diagnosis by proposing an automated system for lesion segmentation and melanoma detection using deep learning. The authors employed an ensemble of deep convolutional neural networks, specifically Inception-v4, ResNet-152, and DenseNet-161, for classification tasks, alongside U-Net architectures for segmentation. The models were trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Challenge dataset, which included 2,000 training images. The proposed method achieved an AUC of 0.867 for melanoma classification using DenseNet-161 and ranked 5th in classification and 8th in segmentation among international teams. Additionally, a traditional XGBoost classifier was tested, yielding a recall of 44.44% and specificity of 69.36%. The results suggest that deep learning ensembles have significant potential to assist clinical specialists by reducing manual segmentation efforts and diagnostic uncertainty.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for automated detection and classification. The study used the ISIC 2017 dataset which contained 374 melanoma training images, 30 validation images, and 117 test images. Deep learning models (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161) and XGBoost were trained to classify melanoma vs non-melanoma.

Secondary target for classification. The study performed specific classification for seborrheic keratosis using the same deep learning models. The ISIC 2017 dataset contained 254 seborrheic keratosis training images, 42 validation images, and 90 test images.
Used as the negative class for melanoma detection tasks. The XGBoost model achieved a specificity of 69.36% in identifying non-melanoma lesions correctly.

The study focused on segmenting the lesion area from the surrounding skin in dermoscopic images. The ground truth masks provided by ISIC were used for training and evaluating segmentation models (U-Net, U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder).

all_task_types.target: Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis vs. Non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification for melanoma detection (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and seborrheic keratosis detection using deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161) and XGBoost. Evaluation metrics include AUC, Accuracy, Recall, and Specificity.

The paper performs pixel-wise segmentation of skin lesions using U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder architectures. The output is a segmentation mask evaluated using Jaccard Index and Dice Coefficient.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Challenge dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing dermoscopic images and corresponding lesion masks labeled by clinical specialists. It includes training, validation, and hold-out test sets.

use.training: Used for training deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161) for melanoma and seborrheic keratosis classification, and U-Net architectures for lesion segmentation. Also used for training XGBoost classifier with extracted features.
use.validation: Used for validation during model development (150 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of classification performance (AUC, Accuracy) and segmentation performance (Jaccard Index, Dice Coefficient) on 600 hold-out test images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To interrupt training after 8 consecutive epochs of non-improvement in the computed AUC on the validation dataset, selecting weights from the best AUC.
To combine predictions from multiple models (U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder) for segmentation by blending masks using a weighted formula (alpha=0.5) to improve generalization.
To measure the performance of classification models outputting probability values between 0 and 1.
Used as the loss function for segmentation training and as an evaluation metric to compare similarity between target and predicted masks.
Used as an evaluation metric to quantify the percent overlap between the target mask and predicted mask.
Used as the primary evaluation metric for classification performance (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis) and for early stopping criteria.
Used as an additional evaluation metric for classification performance.
Used to evaluate the performance of the XGBoost classifier for melanoma detection.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as AUC, Accuracy, Jaccard Index, and Dice Coefficient, and results were compared via ranking in a challenge.

Melanoma Classification.notes: DenseNet-161 achieved the highest AUC and ranked 4th out of 23 submissions. The paper also reports Accuracy values: Inception-v4 (0.820), ResNet-152 (0.837), DenseNet-161 (0.863).

Seborrheic Keratosis Classification.notes: ResNet-152 achieved the highest AUC and ranked 7th out of 23 submissions. The paper also reports Accuracy values: Inception-v4 (0.878), ResNet-152 (0.893), DenseNet-161 (0.887).

Lesion Segmentation.notes: The ensemble model (blending U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16 encoder with alpha=0.5) achieved the highest scores. The model ranked 8th in segmentation among 21 international teams.

XGBoost Classification.notes: XGBoost was trained on resized (32x32) grayscale images with added metadata (age, gender). It achieved a low F1-score due to low recall. The paper notes that deep learning models outperformed this traditional approach.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161 for classification; U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder for segmentation; XGBoost for traditional classification
architecture_used.pretraining: Classification models (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, DenseNet-161) initialized with weights pre-trained on ImageNet. U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder used VGG-16 layers up to the last convolutional layer pre-trained on ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: Classification: Resized to 224x224 for DenseNet and ResNet; 299x299 for Inception-v4. Segmentation: Not explicitly specified, but images resized to max 1024x1024 prior to processing. XGBoost: 32x32 grayscale images.

architecture_details.u_net_encoder_details: VGG-16 layers up to the last convolutional layer (13 convolutional layers with ReLU, 5 max-pooling operations). Decoder: 4 upsampling operations and 8 convolutional layers.
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Weighted blending of segmentation masks: g(x) = (1 - α)f1(x) + αf2(x) with α = 0.5.

training_details.learning_rate: Initialized at 1e-3, lowered to 1e-4 after 10th epoch
training_details.early_stopping: Interrupted training after 8 consecutive epochs of non-improvement in AUC on validation set
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotations up to 270 degrees, random online augmentations using torchvision and imgaug
training_details.normalization: Normalized based on ImageNet dataset statistics

classification_or_output_head.classification_head: Binary classification output (probability between 0 and 1) for melanoma vs non-melanoma and seborrheic keratosis vs non-melanoma

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Training images resized to 128x128 for segmentation models; resized to 224x224 for DenseNet and ResNet classification; resized to 299x299 for Inception-v4 classification; resized to 32x32 for XGBoost classification.
Data Augmentation: Online image augmentation including horizontal and vertical flips, zooms up to 20%, and rotations up to 270 degrees.
Normalization: Images normalized based on ImageNet dataset statistics for deep learning models.
Grayscale Conversion: Images converted to grayscale for XGBoost model (though noted to reduce accuracy).
Contrast Adjustment: Contrast brightened to enhance color intensity for XGBoost model.
Metadata Integration: Patient age (imputed with mean if missing) and gender (one-hot encoded) added as features for XGBoost model.
Histogram Analysis: RGB pixel value histogramming (0-767 range) applied for XGBoost feature extraction.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training using torchvision and imgaug libraries. Included horizontal and vertical flips, zooms up to 20%, and rotations up to 270 degrees.
AUC value was computed through the mean of predictions for 16 randomly augmented copies of each validation image. These augmentations followed similar transformations used during training.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning ensemble approach effectively minimizes uncertainty and subjectivity in melanoma diagnosis by automating lesion segmentation and detection.

The ensemble of deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v4, ResNet-152, and DenseNet-161) demonstrated strong performance, with DenseNet-161 achieving the highest AUC of 0.867 for melanoma classification and ranking 4th among 23 submissions.
For seborrheic keratosis classification, ResNet-152 achieved the highest AUC of 0.918, ranking 7th among 23 submissions.
The U-Net ensemble model for lesion segmentation achieved a Jaccard Index of 0.747 and a Dice Coefficient of 0.834, ranking 8th among 21 international teams.

Ensembling segmentation masks from different models (U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16 Encoder) improved generalization by mimicking the clinical practice of specialists seeking second opinions, particularly in cases where ground truth labels were overly broad or ambiguous.
The traditional XGBoost classifier performed poorly (Recall: 44.44%, Specificity: 69.36%) compared to deep learning models, indicating that deep learning is more effective for this task.
Converting images to grayscale and relying on histogram features for XGBoost reduced accuracy, highlighting the importance of color information in lesion classification.

The study acknowledges that ground truth labels in the dataset can be inconsistent (e.g., drawing large masks around small lesions), which may penalize accurate model predictions and suggests that finer-grained labeling could improve performance.
The proposed method has significant potential to assist clinical specialists by reducing manual segmentation efforts and diagnostic uncertainty.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges significant inconsistency and subjectivity in the ground truth labels provided by clinical specialists, noting that masks are often drawn as 'large circles' including normal skin, which penalizes models that predict tighter, more accurate lesion boundaries.

The XGBoost traditional machine learning approach performed poorly (Recall: 44.44%, Specificity: 69.36%) compared to deep learning models, highlighting the limitations of hand-crafted features and grayscale conversion for this task.
The paper notes that converting images to grayscale reduced accuracy, emphasizing the importance of color information in lesion classification.
The study identifies a limitation where small lesions with high visual similarity to melanoma are difficult for classifiers to detect due to insufficient feature extraction from small areas.

The ensemble segmentation method (blending U-Net and U-Net with VGG-16) improved generalization by mimicking clinical practice of seeking second opinions, particularly in cases of ambiguous ground truth.

The models were evaluated on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which is a public challenge dataset; results are ranked against other international teams (5th in classification, 8th in segmentation) rather than compared against a specific clinical baseline or external independent cohort.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values) was performed; performance was evaluated using AUC, Accuracy, Jaccard Index, and Dice Coefficient.
